function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---

results.content:
Based on the provided text, here is all the information regarding the 2026 stock split:

*   **Split Ratio:** The split was a five-for-one (5-for-1) forward stock split. Each share of Class A, Class B, and Class C Common Stock issued and outstanding was split into five shares of the same class.
*   **Effective Date:** The split was effective on May 4, 2026 (approved by the Board in May 2026).
*   **Affected Shares:** The split applied to the authorized, issued, and outstanding shares of the Company's Class A, Class B, and Class C Common Stock.
*   **Effect on Per-Share Data:** All share and per-share information included in the prospectus has been retroactively adjusted to reflect the 2026 Stock Split for all periods presented.
*   **Effect on Preferred Stock:** The conversion rate of SpaceX Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock was proportionately adjusted to factor in the split. Following the split, each share of SpaceX Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock is convertible into fifty shares of SpaceX Common Stock.
*   **Effect on Mergers and Exchange Ratios:** The conversion mechanics and exchange ratios resulting from the xAI Merger (such as the 0.1433 shares of SpaceX stock per xAI share ratio and the $75.46 cash election per share) are explicitly noted to be on a "pre-2026 Stock Split basis."
*   **Auditor's Report:** The independent auditor's report notes an exception date of May 7, 2026, specifically to account for the effects of the stock split discussed in Note 1 of the consolidated financial statements.
*   **Rationale:** The provided text does not contain any discussion regarding the rationale or reasoning behind the 2026 stock split.

Based on the provided text, the 2026 Stock Split is reflected in historical per-share data through the following adjustments:

*   **Retroactive Adjustment of All Share and Per Share Information:** The Company effected a five-for-one forward stock split of its Class A, Class B, and Class C Common Stock on May 4, 2026. Unless otherwise stated, all share and per share information included in the prospectus has been retroactively adjusted to reflect this 2026 Stock Split for all periods presented.
*   **Proportionate Adjustment to Conversion Rates:** The conversion rate of SpaceX Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock was proportionately adjusted to factor in the 2026 Stock Split. 
*   **Retrospective Recasting of Financial Statements:** The consolidated financial statements of SpaceX have been retrospectively recast for all periods presented to include the five-for-one stock split (along with the xAI and X mergers, which were between entities under common control). 
*   **Impact on Weighted Average Shares:** As a result of the retroactive adjustments, the historical weighted average shares used in computing net income (loss) per share of common stock (both basic and diluted) reflect the adjusted share counts. For example, the basic and diluted weighted average shares outstanding for the year ended December 31, 2025, are shown as 2,926 million, and for the year ended December 31, 2023, they are shown as 2,759 million.

### 1. IPO Price Range & Number of Shares Offered
While the specific numbers are redacted (blanks) in the main prospectus text, the S-1 fee table provides explicit figures:
*   **Number of Shares Offered:** **66,666,670** shares of Class A common stock.

*   **Over-Allotment Option:** The underwriters have a 30-day option to purchase up to an additional (redacted) number of shares of Class A common stock.

### 2. Underwriters
The IPO is being underwritten by a large syndicate of financial institutions. The lead bookrunning managers and co-managers include:
*   **Lead Underwriters:** Goldman Sachs & Co. LLC, Morgan Stanley & Co. LLC, BofA Securities, Inc., Citigroup Global Markets Inc., and J.P. Morgan Securities LLC.
*   **Additional Underwriters:** Barclays Capital Inc., Deutsche Bank Securities Inc., RBC Capital Markets, LLC, UBS Securities LLC, Wells Fargo Securities, LLC, Banco BTG Pactual S.A. – Cayman Branch, ING Bank N.V., Allen & Company LLC, Cantor, Needham & Company, Raymond James, Societe Generale, Stifel, William Blair, Macquarie Capital, Mirae Asset Securities Co., Ltd., Mizuho, and Santander.
*   *Note:* ING Bank N.V., Banco BTG Pactual S.A. – Cayman Branch, and Mirae Asset Securities Co., Ltd. are noted as not being broker-dealers registered with the SEC, restricting their direct sales in the U.S. 

### 3. Offering Structure
The offering features several distinct structural components:
*   **Dual-Class Voting Structure:** The company will have Class A and Class B common stock. Class A shareholders get 1 vote per share, while Class B shareholders get 10 votes per share. Class B shareholders will vote together with Class A as a single class, except they have the exclusive right to elect a majority of the board of directors.
*   **Pre-Offering Reclassifications:** Prior to the IPO, all outstanding Class C common stock will be reclassified into Class A common stock, and all preferred stock will convert into Class A and Class B common stock.
*   **Directed Share Program (DSP):** Up to a (redacted) percent of shares are reserved for sale to certain employees, management associates, friends, and family. These shares will not be subject to lock-up restrictions.
*   **Retail Investor Allocation:** A portion of shares will be offered to retail investors through specific online brokerage platforms, including Charles Schwab, Fidelity, Robinhood, SoFi Securities, and E*TRADE.

*   **Stabilization:** Morgan Stanley will act as stabilization agent and may engage in covered and naked short sales to stabilize the price of the Class A common stock post-IPO.

The prospectus details the specific online brokerage platforms that will be used to offer shares to retail investors:

> "In addition to allocations made to retail investors by the underwriters, we currently anticipate that certain of the shares of Class A common stock offered hereby will, at our request, be offered to retail investors through Charles Schwab & Co., Inc., Fidelity Brokerage Services LLC and Fidelity Capital Markets, a division of National Financial Services LLC, Robinhood Financial, LLC, and SoFi Securities LLC, as selling group members, via their respective online brokerage platforms. The document outlines the DSP, noting that shares reserved for employees and management associates will not be subject to lock-up restrictions:

> "At our request, the underwriters have reserved up to percent of the shares of Class A common stock offered by this prospectus for sale at the initial public offering price through a directed share program to certain employees and persons identified by our management, which may include parties with whom we have a business relationship and friends and family of management. If purchased by these persons, these shares will not be subject to a lock-up restriction. The number of shares of Class A common stock available for sale to the general public will be reduced by the number of reserved shares sold to these persons. Any reserved shares not purchased by these persons will be offered by the underwriters to the general public on the same basis as the other shares of Class A common stock offered by this prospectus. We will agree to indemnify the underwriters and their affiliates against certain liabilities and expenses, including liabilities under the Securities Act, in connection with sales of the shares reserved for the directed share program. Morgan Stanley & Co. LLC, an underwriter in this offering, and its affiliates will administer the global directed share program."

The text details standard lock-up periods of 180 days and 366 days, with specific staggered early release provisions and strict prohibitions on hedging or transferring shares.

> "We and all of our directors and executive officers have agreed not to sell any shares of Class A common stock for a period of days after the date of this prospectus, subject to certain exceptions... In addition, all of the remaining shares of our common stock, and securities convertible into, exchangeable for or that represent the right to receive our common stock, are subject to a variety of other terms governing restrictions on the sale, short sale, transfer, hedging, pledging, or other disposition of their interests in our equity, pursuant to a number of different agreements, for 180 days from the date of this prospectus."

> "Our Founder and certain significant investors have agreed with the underwriters, that during a period of 366 days after the date of this prospectus, an aggregate of shares owned by them (including 100% of the shares owned by our Founder), representing % of our shares outstanding, are subject to the restrictions described in the paragraph below. ## The 2026 Stock Split: Rationales and Reflection in Historical Per-Share Data

### 1. What the Filing Discloses About the Split

The SpaceX S-1 (filed May 20, 2026) discloses the following about the "2026 Stock Split" in Note 1 to the consolidated financial statements:

> "On May 4, 2026, the Company effected a five-for-one forward stock split of its authorized, issued, and outstanding shares of Class A, Class B, and Class C Common Stock ('2026 Stock Split'). The conversion rate of SpaceX Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock was proportionately adjusted to factor in the 2026 Stock Split. All share and per share information has been retroactively adjusted to reflect the 2026 Stock Split for all periods presented."

Key mechanical facts:
- **Ratio:** 5-for-1 forward split
- **Effective date:** May 4, 2026 (approximately two weeks before the S-1 filing date of May 20, 2026)
- **Classes affected:** Class A, Class B, and Class C Common Stock (authorized, issued, and outstanding)
- **Preferred stock:** Conversion rate proportionately adjusted — post-split, each share of SpaceX Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock converts into 50 shares of common stock
- **Auditor emphasis:** The independent auditor's report carries an explanatory paragraph with an exception date of May 7, 2026, specifically referencing the stock split discussed in Note 1

### 2. Typical Underwriting and Market-Structure Rationales for a Pre-IPO Split

**Important disclosure caveat:** The S-1 does **not** explicitly state the rationale for the 2026 Stock Split. The filing describes only the mechanical effects. The rationales below reflect standard underwriting and market-structure practice for forward stock splits executed shortly before an IPO, supported by the offering structure disclosed elsewhere in the prospectus.

**(a) Retail investor accessibility and affordability.** A forward split reduces the per-share price by the split ratio (here, dividing by 5), making individual shares more affordable to retail investors who may trade in whole shares rather than fractional units. This is particularly relevant for SpaceX, whose prospectus expressly provides for a **retail investor allocation** through online brokerage platforms — Charles Schwab, Fidelity, Robinhood, SoFi Securities, and E*TRADE — at the same IPO price as institutional allocations. A lower post-split price broadens the addressable retail order book.

**(b) Board-lot and round-lot alignment.** U.S. exchanges traditionally quote and trade in round lots of 100 shares. A high nominal pre-split price can make a single round lot prohibitively expensive for many retail accounts. Splitting before the IPO aligns the post-split price with conventional round-lot economics, improving order flow granularity.

**(c) Liquidity and share-count depth.** A 5-for-1 split multiplies the float fivefold. More shares outstanding at a lower price typically supports tighter bid-ask spreads, greater depth at each price level, and higher share-volume turnover — all of which underwriters value for post-IPO trading stability and for the stabilization activities disclosed in the prospectus (Morgan Stanley as stabilization agent).

**(d) Conventional IPO pricing band.** Underwriters generally prefer an offering price within a range that is familiar to the market and compatible with exchange listing standards. A split executed before pricing allows the syndicate to set the IPO price on a post-split basis, avoiding an anomalously high nominal price that could deter participation or create odd-lot fragmentation.

**(e) Option and equity-compensation granularity.** A lower per-share price permits finer-grained option strike prices and RSU grant sizing, which is relevant given SpaceX's large employee equity program and the Directed Share Program (DSP) disclosed in the prospectus for employees, management associates, friends, and family.

**(f) Index and eligibility considerations.** Certain index inclusion methodologies and exchange listing criteria reference per-share price thresholds. A split can position the post-IPO stock within conventional price bands for index eligibility over time.

### 3. How the Filing Reflects the Split in Historical Per-Share Data

The filing applies **retrospective retroactive adjustment** to all historical per-share data, consistent with ASC 260 (Earnings Per Share) and ASC 505 (Equity) treatment of stock splits. Specifically:

**(a) Universal retroactive adjustment.** The prospectus preamble states:

> "The consolidated financial statements of SpaceX have been retrospectively recast for all periods presented to include (i) the historical results of X.AI Holdings Corp.... and X Holdings Corp.... because these transactions were between entities under common control, and (ii) a five-for-one stock split of the Company's Class A, Class B, and Class C Common Stock, effective May 4, 2026 (the '2026 Stock Split'). Unless otherwise stated or the context otherwise requires, all share and per share information included in this prospectus have been retroactively adjusted to reflect the 2026 Stock Split."

This means every share count, per-share metric, weighted-average share figure, and conversion/exchange ratio in the filing is presented on a post-split (5×) basis for all historical periods, unless explicitly noted otherwise.

**(b) Weighted-average shares outstanding (post-split basis, in millions):**

| Period | Basic | Diluted |
|--------|-------|---------|
| FY 2023 | 2,759 | 2,759 |
| FY 2024 | 2,848 | 9,956 |
| FY 2025 | 2,926 | 2,926 |

The 2024 diluted figure (9,956 million) is elevated because 2024 was a net-income year, so dilutive securities — primarily the conversion of SpaceX Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock (6,771 million shares), plus stock options (292 million) and RSUs (45 million) — were included. In loss years (2023, 2025), potentially dilutive securities were anti-dilutive and excluded, so basic equals diluted. All of these share counts reflect the 5× post-split adjustment.

**(c) Net income (loss) per share (post-split basis):**

| Period | Basic | Diluted |
|--------|-------|---------|
| FY 2023 | $(1.68) | $(1.68) |
| FY 2024 | $0.01 | $0.00 |
| FY 2025 | $(1.69) | $(1.69) |

These per-share figures are computed using the retroactively adjusted (post-split) weighted-average share counts.

**(d) Preferred stock conversion rate.** The conversion rate of SpaceX Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock was proportionately adjusted. Post-split, each share of preferred converts into 50 shares of common stock (i.e., 5× the pre-split conversion rate of 10).

**(e) Merger exchange ratios — explicit "pre-split" carve-out.** The filing explicitly flags that certain merger mechanics remain on a pre-split basis. The xAI Merger exchange ratio (0.1433 shares of SpaceX stock per xAI share) and the $75.46 cash election per share are noted as being on a "pre-2026 Stock Split basis." This is the principal exception to the universal retroactive adjustment and is disclosed to avoid confusion when comparing merger consideration to post-split share counts.

**(f) Auditor's report emphasis.** The independent auditor's report includes an emphasis-of-matter paragraph with an exception date of May 7, 2026, drawing attention to the stock split effects described in Note 1 — a standard procedure when a significant non-recurring capital-structure event occurs between the balance-sheet date and the audit report date.

### 4. Summary

The 2026 Stock Split was a 5-for-1 forward split effective May 4, 2026, executed approximately two weeks before the S-1 filing. While the filing does **not** explicitly state the rationale, the offering structure — including a dedicated retail allocation through major online brokerages, a Directed Share Program, and stabilization arrangements — is consistent with the standard underwriting and market-structure rationales for a pre-IPO split: retail affordability, round-lot alignment, liquidity enhancement, conventional pricing bands, and equity-compensation granularity. The filing reflects the split by retroactively adjusting all share and per-share information for all periods presented, with weighted-average shares and EPS figures restated on a post-split basis, the preferred-stock conversion rate proportionately adjusted, and merger exchange ratios explicitly carved out as remaining on a pre-split basis.
